name: Rayta bint al-Harith
title: Rayta bint al-Harith (RA): She Set Out for Madinah
meta_description: The story of Rayta bint al-Harith (RA), an early believer who migrated twice for her faith and died on the road to Madinah with three of her children. A quiet life with a reward promised by Allah.
keywords: Rayta bint al-Harith, story of Rayta, early Muslims, migration to Abyssinia, hijra to Madinah, sahaba stories, women of the sahaba
lede: Some of the people the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ loved most are people we have never heard of. They left no famous sayings and won no famous battles. They simply believed early, gave everything, and trusted Allah with the rest.

This is the story of Rayta bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her): one of the first to accept Islam, a mother who carried her faith across two migrations, and a woman who died on the road before she ever reached the home she was travelling toward.

paras: Rayta and her husband, al-Harith ibn Khalid, accepted Islam very early. They were among the first forty or fifty people to follow the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, in the years when believing was still a private and dangerous thing.

We know very little about them in those Makkan days. The record does not tell us how they came to faith, or what they had to leave behind for it. What we know is that they were there at the beginning, when the number of believers could still be counted on a few hands.

They were not from the powerful houses of Makkah. They held no high rank, commanded no large tribe, and could not count on anyone to protect them. They were a young couple with little standing and a great deal of faith.

This was the shape of so many of the earliest Muslims. Many of them were young, vulnerable, and easy to overlook. Some had been slaves. The message reached the people the city had pushed to its edges, and they answered it before it was safe or popular to do so.

When the persecution grew heavy, a group of believers left Makkah for Abyssinia, where a just Christian king gave them refuge. They travelled under the leadership of Jafar ibn Abi Talib. Rayta and her husband were among them.

It was the vulnerable who made that journey: the young couples and the disenfranchised, people without a powerful tribe to shield them. Rayta and al-Harith fit that picture exactly. They had no rank to lose and every reason to leave.

They had no children when they left Makkah. They arrived in a foreign land with almost nothing, and they waited there, anxiously, for word from the Prophet ﷺ about whether to return to Makkah or move somewhere else.

While they waited, their family grew. In Abyssinia, Rayta gave birth to four children: Musa, Zainab, Aisha, and Fatima. Other families who made that journey returned with one child, or two. A couple who had crossed the sea with empty arms now had a household full of small lives.

Then the news came that the Prophet ﷺ had migrated to Madinah, and that a new community of believers was gathering there. For people who had spent years far from home, waiting for exactly this word, it was the news they had been praying for.

Rayta and her husband did not wait. They were among those who left Abyssinia at once, with their four children, to join the Muslims in Madinah. Ahead of them lay the promise they had held onto through years of exile: to finally live among their own people, under the Prophet ﷺ himself, in a city that belonged to faith.

A journey like that, with little provision, asks hard questions at every stage. Where do you stop. How do you pace yourself. What is there to eat and drink along the way. These are not small worries on a long road. You prepare as well as you can, and then you place the rest in the hands of Allah.

We can only imagine the mood of that family as they travelled. After everything, they were going home, even though it was a home none of them had seen. Every mile brought them closer to the thing they had hoped for.

As the family drew close to Madinah, they stopped to rest. They drank from some water they found on the way, and then they lay down to sleep, almost within reach of the city they had crossed the world to enter.

Al-Harith woke to a scene that is hard to hold in the mind. The water had been tainted. His wife, Rayta, and three of his four children were dead. There was no enemy and no cruelty in it. They had simply drunk bad water on the last stretch of a long road, and it had taken almost his whole family in their sleep.

It is the kind of loss that resists explanation. There was no battle to make sense of it, no oppressor to name. The excitement of arriving, the promise of Madinah, the years of waiting, all of it turned in a single morning into something he could barely take in.

He carried on into Madinah with the one daughter who survived, Fatima. He had left Abyssinia with a wife and four children. He arrived with a single child, and the rest of his family buried behind him on the way. He would go on to keep the company of the Prophet ﷺ for the rest of his life.

sections.h2: A reward that was already theirs
paras: Rayta and her children had set out for the sake of Allah and died before they arrived. They were among the muhajirin, those who migrated, and they are counted among the shuhada, those who gave their lives on that path.

Theirs was not even a single migration. They had moved from Makkah to Abyssinia, and then set out from Abyssinia to Madinah. They died travelling from one hijra to another, for no reason other than their faith. It is hard to even put their standing with Allah into words.

There were others like them in those years, and the community in Madinah began to wonder about them. A man named Khalid, the younger brother of Hakim and a relative of Khadijah, set out for the hijra and died of a snakebite on the way. Some had intended to migrate and were stopped, or persecuted, or killed, before they could complete it.

It was about such people, the ones whose intention outran their lives, that Allah sent down a clear promise. They were not to think their effort had been lost, or that they had fallen short in the eyes of their Lord:

ayah.text: Anyone who migrates for God's cause will find many a refuge and great plenty in the earth, and if anyone leaves home as a migrant towards God and His Messenger and is then overtaken by death, his reward from God is sure. God is most forgiving and most merciful.

sections.h2: When death is hard to make sense of
paras: There is a reason a story like this stays with us. We have all watched loss arrive without warning, and seen families lose more than one person at once. It can be very hard to come to terms with deaths like these, and faith does not ask us to pretend otherwise.

What Rayta's story offers is not an explanation but a reassurance. Her family did not die in vain, and they did not die outside the care of Allah. The same decree that took them had already secured their reward. The loss was real, and so was the mercy holding it.

For al-Harith, left with one daughter, there was no neat answer either. He went forward, stayed close to the Prophet ﷺ, and carried his grief into a life of faith. Sometimes that is what trust looks like: not understanding why, but continuing to walk toward Allah anyway.

It is worth sitting with how ordinary the cause of this loss was. Not a sword, not a famine, not an act of malice. Just water that had gone bad on a journey, the sort of small danger anyone might meet. And through it, an entire family entered the ranks of those whose reward is guaranteed. The smallest of causes, in the hands of Allah, opened the largest of doors.

Rayta is not a name most Muslims have heard. Neither are the names of her children. They do not appear in the famous stories, and for centuries their journey has been a quiet line in a long history. Many of us learn it for the first time and realise we never knew they existed.

Yet think of where they may stand on the Day of Judgement. Their intention was sincere, and their effort was complete. They gave up their homes twice and their lives once, and the reward they were travelling toward was already written for them. The people Makkah overlooked may be among the highest in the sight of Allah.

Allah promised that He would not let the reward of such people go to waste. Everything they hoped to find in Madinah, their intention had already earned them. They never arrived, and yet, in the sight of Allah, they had already arrived.

lessons_intro: Her story is short and almost entirely hidden, and that is part of its lesson. A life can be unknown to people and precious to Allah.

lessons.bold: Intention carries weight, even when the work is unfinished.
lessons.text: Rayta died before reaching Madinah, yet the reward of the journey was hers. Allah measures the heart that set out, not only the destination reached.

lessons.bold: Faith often belongs to the overlooked.
lessons.text: She was not powerful or famous. She was a young woman from a small household who believed early and gave everything, and that was enough.

lessons.bold: Not every loss carries a reason we can see.
lessons.text: Her family drank bad water on the road. There was no enemy in it, only a hard decree, and her example helps us trust Allah when there is nothing to blame.

lessons.bold: Allah does not waste what is done for Him.
lessons.text: A life spent moving toward Him is never spent in vain, even when it ends short of the place it was aiming for.

faq.a: She was one of the earliest believers in Islam, among the first forty or fifty to follow the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. With her husband, al-Harith ibn Khalid, she migrated to Abyssinia and later set out for Madinah, where she died on the way.

faq.q: How did Rayta and her children die?
faq.a: On the final stretch of the journey from Abyssinia to Madinah, the family drank from tainted water and slept. Rayta and three of her four children did not wake. It appears to have been spoiled water, with no enemy or cruelty involved.

Because she died as a migrant for the sake of Allah, travelling from one hijra to another. The Quran (4:100) promises that whoever leaves home for Allah and is overtaken by death has a sure reward, and her family is counted among them.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Rayta?
faq.a: That a sincere intention is rewarded even when the work is left unfinished, that faith often belongs to the unnoticed, and that Allah never wastes a life given for His sake.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will live lives that no history book records. We make quiet intentions, set out toward something good, and sometimes never see it finished. Rayta's story is a gentle answer to the fear that this is not enough. The One who counts our deeds was watching the whole road, not only its end, and the effort of the heart is never wasted with Him.

Her life invites us to set out anyway: to begin the prayer, the change, the journey of the heart, and to trust that the trying itself is seen and kept. It also invites us to hold our losses with a little more gentleness, knowing that what looks like an ending to us may already be a reward with Allah. May Allah grant us sincere intentions, and reward us for the good we reach for even when we fall short of reaching it.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Rayta bint al-Harith (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 4:100). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
Some of the people the Prophet ﷺ loved most are people we have never heard of.

Rayta bint al-Harith (RA) was one of them.

She and her husband believed early.

They were among the first forty or fifty Muslims, in the years when faith was a private and dangerous thing.

They were not powerful or famous.

A young couple, no high rank, no tribe to protect them. Just faith, and the courage to act on it.

When the persecution grew heavy, they left Makkah for Abyssinia.

They arrived with empty arms. There, they were given four children: Musa, Zainab, Aisha, and Fatima.

Then came the news: the Prophet ﷺ had reached Madinah.

They set out at once, the whole family, toward the home they had prayed for.

Near the city, they drank some water and slept.

The water was tainted. Rayta and three of her children did not wake. Her husband arrived in Madinah with one daughter.

They died as migrants, travelling from one hijra to another.

Allah revealed: whoever leaves home for Him and is overtaken by death, "his reward from God is sure." (Quran 4:100)

**Allah does not waste what is done for Him.**

They never reached Madinah. In the sight of Allah, they had already arrived.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
Some of the companions the Prophet ﷺ loved most left no famous sayings and won no famous battles. Rayta bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her) is one of them.

She believed early, when it was dangerous to. She migrated to Abyssinia, raised four children far from home, and then set out for Madinah the moment she heard the Prophet ﷺ had arrived there. On the last stretch of the road, the family drank tainted water in their sleep, and Rayta and three of her children passed away before reaching the city.

They died travelling for the sake of Allah, and the Quran promises that such a reward is never lost. She never arrived, and yet she had already arrived.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/rayta-bint-al-harith

email.subject: She set out for Madinah and never arrived
email.alt_subject: The companion you have never heard of
email.preview: Rayta believed early, migrated twice, and died on the last stretch of the road.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the people the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ loved most are people we have never heard of. They left no famous sayings and won no famous battles. They simply believed early, gave everything, and trusted Allah with the rest.

Rayta bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her) was one of them. She and her husband accepted Islam very early, among the first forty or fifty believers, in the years when faith was still a private and dangerous thing. They were not powerful or well known. They were a young couple with little standing and a great deal of faith.

When the persecution in Makkah grew heavy, they migrated to Abyssinia and took refuge under a just king. They had arrived with empty arms, and there they were given four children: Musa, Zainab, Aisha, and Fatima.

Then the news reached them that the Prophet ﷺ had migrated to Madinah, and that a new community was gathering there. They did not wait. The whole family set out at once, toward the home they had been praying for.

Near the city, they stopped to rest. They drank from some water they found, and lay down to sleep. The water was tainted. Rayta and three of her four children did not wake. Her husband carried on into Madinah with the one daughter who survived, the rest of his family buried behind him on the road.

They had set out for the sake of Allah and died before they arrived. About people like them, Allah sent down a promise: whoever leaves home as a migrant toward Him and is overtaken by death, his reward is sure with Allah.

There is a quiet comfort in her story. Most of us will live lives that no one records, making intentions and setting out toward good things we may never see finished. Rayta reminds us that the One who counts our deeds is watching the whole road, not only its end. She never reached Madinah, and yet, in the sight of Allah, she had already arrived.

May Allah be pleased with her, and reward us for the good we reach for even when we fall short of it.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some of the people the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ loved most are people we have never heard of. Rayta bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her) was one of them.

She and her husband accepted Islam very early, among the first forty or fifty believers, when faith was still a private and dangerous thing. They held no rank and led no tribe. They were a young couple with little standing and a great deal of faith.

When Makkah turned cruel, they migrated to Abyssinia and found refuge under a just king. They had crossed the sea with empty arms, and there they were given four children: Musa, Zainab, Aisha, and Fatima.
Then the news arrived that the Prophet ﷺ had reached Madinah. They did not wait. The whole family set out at once, toward the home they had spent years praying for.

Near the city, they stopped to rest, drank from some water they found, and slept. The water was tainted. Rayta and three of her children did not wake. Her husband carried on into Madinah with one surviving daughter, the rest of his family buried behind him on the road.
They had set out for the sake of Allah and died before they arrived. They were migrants who passed away travelling from one hijra to another, and the Quran promises that such a reward is never lost.

Their names are not in the famous stories. Yet everything they hoped to find in Madinah, their intention had already earned them. They never arrived, and in the sight of Allah they had already arrived.
storybook.pullquote: She never reached Madinah, and in the sight of Allah she had already arrived.
storybook.reflection: Most of us will live lives that no history records. We make quiet intentions and set out toward good things we may never see finished. Rayta's story is a gentle answer to the fear that this is not enough. Allah was watching the whole road, not only its end, and He does not waste what is done for Him.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
